perturbation.original_text: The ValueAct Designee agrees that, at all times while serving as a member of the Board, he will (i) meet all director independence and other standards of the Company, the New York Stock Exchange and the Securities and Exchange Commission ("SEC") and applicable provisions of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the "Exchange Act"), and the rules and regulations promulgated thereunder, including Rule 10A- 3; and (ii) be qualified to serve as a director under the Delaware General Corporation Law (the "DGCL") (clauses (i) and (ii), the "Conditions").
perturbation.changed_text: The ValueAct Designee agrees that, at all times while serving as a member of the Board, he will (ii) be qualified to serve as a director under the Delaware General Corporation Law (the "DGCL") (clauses (i) and (ii), the "Conditions").
perturbation.explanation: By removing clause (i), the contract no longer explicitly requires the ValueAct Designee to meet all director independence standards of the SEC and NYSE, potentially violating regulations around director independence and audit committee composition.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts 17 CFR § 240.10A-3, which mandates specific independence requirements for audit committee members of listed companies to ensure objective oversight. Omitting the requirement that the ValueAct Designee meet these independence standards could lead to non-compliance if the designee serves on the audit committee and does not, in fact, meet the independence criteria.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10A-3 - Listing standards relating to audit committees. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10A-3 - Listing standards relating to audit committees. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.10A-3 Listing standards relating to audit committees. (a) Pursuant to section 10A(m) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78j-1(m) ) and section 3 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 ( 15 U.S.C. 7202 ): (1) National securities exchanges. The rules of each national securities exchange registered pursuant to section 6 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78f ) must, in accordance with the provisions of this section , prohibit the initial or continued listing of any security of an issuer that is not in compliance with the requirements of any portion of paragraph (b) or (c) of this section . (2) National securities associations. The rules of each national securities association registered pursuant to section 15A of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78o-3 ) must, in accordance with the provisions of this section , prohibit the initial or continued listing in an automated inter-dealer quotation system of any security of an issuer that is not in compliance with the requirements of any portion of paragraph (b) or (c) of this section . (3) Opportunity to cure defects. The rules required by paragraphs (a)(1) and (a)(2) of this section must provide for appropriate procedures for a listed issuer to have an opportunity to cure any defects that would be the basis for a prohibition under paragraph (a) of this section , before the imposition of such prohibition. Such rules also may provide that if a member of an audit committee ceases to be independent in accordance with the requirements of this section for reasons outside the member 's reasonable control , that person, with notice by the issuer to the applicable national securities exchange or national securities association, may remain an audit committee m
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the explicit requirement for the ValueAct Designee to meet director independence standards of the SEC and NYSE. The Law Explanation states that this contradicts 17 CFR § 240.10A-3, which mandates independence requirements for audit committee members. Scraped Law Snippet 2 explicitly mentions 17 CFR § 240.10A-3 and its relation to listing standards relating to audit committees, referencing section 10A(m) of the Act (15 U.S.C. 78j-1(m)). This section requires national securities exchanges and associations to prohibit the listing of any security of an issuer that does not comply with the requirements of paragraph (b) or (c) of this section. By removing the clause that ensures compliance with independence standards, the modified contract creates a significant risk of non-compliance with these listing standards, thus creating a legal contradiction and undermining director independence regulations.

perturbation.original_text: At all times while serving as a member of the Board, the ValueAct Designee shall comply with all policies, procedures, processes, codes, rules, standards and guidelines applicable to Board members, including the Company's Code of Business Conduct, Insider Trading Policy, Executive Stock Ownership Policy as in effect on the date hereof, and Corporate Governance Guidelines, and (except as permitted by the Confidentiality Agreement (as defined in Section 7 below)) preserve the confidentiality of Company business and information, including discussions or matters considered in meetings of the Board or Board committees to the extent not disclosed publicly by the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: At all times while serving as a member of the Board, the ValueAct Designee shall comply with all policies, procedures, processes, codes, rules, standards and guidelines applicable to Board members, including the Company's Code of Business Conduct and Corporate Governance Guidelines, and (except as permitted by the Confidentiality Agreement (as defined in Section 7 below)) preserve the confidentiality of Company business and information, including discussions or matters considered in meetings of the Board or Board committees to the extent not disclosed publicly by the Company.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the reference to the 'Insider Trading Policy', the agreement weakens the explicit requirement for the ValueAct Designee to adhere to insider trading regulations, potentially increasing the risk of non-compliance and legal issues. While a general code of conduct may exist, the explicit mention is important.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: The omission of the 'Insider Trading Policy' reference weakens the agreement's stance on compliance with insider trading laws, specifically violating Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 (15 U.S. Code § 78j(b)) and Rule 10b-5. These laws prohibit the use of material, non-public information for trading securities. Although the company may have a general insider trading policy, the explicit mention in this agreement reinforces the importance of adhering to those rules, and its removal introduces legal risk.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The omission of the 'Insider Trading Policy' directly weakens the commitment to compliance, creating a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states that Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 (15 U.S. Code § 78j(b)) prohibits using non-public information for trading. The original text explicitly requires compliance with the Insider Trading Policy. By removing this, the changed text creates a loophole that could lead to non-compliance with insider trading laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows the text of 15 USC 78j, which includes manipulative and deceptive devices which can include insider trading. Since the change weakens the explicit requirement, the contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Each of the parties hereto agrees to waive any bonding requirement under any applicable law, in the case any other party seeks to enforce the terms by way of equitable relief.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the parties hereto agrees to waive any bonding requirement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'under any applicable law, in the case any other party seeks to enforce the terms by way of equitable relief,' the contract attempts to create a blanket waiver of bonding requirements, potentially contradicting laws that mandate bonds in certain specific equitable actions, such as preliminary injunctions, to protect the defendant if the injunction is later found to be wrongly issued. A blanket waiver may not be enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 65(c) requires security (a bond) to be given for a preliminary injunction or temporary restraining order. While a court may have discretion to waive the bond in certain circumstances, this blanket waiver attempts to eliminate the possibility of requiring a bond altogether, contradicting the rule's intent to protect parties from potential damages caused by wrongfully issued injunctions. The removal of 'under any applicable law, in the case any other party seeks to enforce the terms by way of equitable relief' broadens the waiver beyond what may be legally permissible.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The original contract language waives bonding requirements only when equitable relief is sought. The changed language broadens this waiver to a blanket waiver of bonding requirements. FRCP 65(c) implies a bond is generally required for preliminary injunctions. By omitting the clause that limited the waiver to specific instances of equitable relief, the changed text now potentially contradicts FRCP 65(c) by attempting to eliminate the bond requirement altogether, a consumer protection, even when legally mandated. This falls under the definition of an Omission Legal Contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions Rule 65 relates to injunctions, suggesting this is the law that is being contradicted.
